behaviors.description: The request sent to the external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that the taxonomy list is incomplete, malformed, or improperly serialized in the actual request payload sent to the external API. A unit mock of the API method could verify we're passing the right parameter, but wouldn't catch serialization issues (JSON encoding, field naming) or how the HTTP client constructs the request body. Integration testing with either a mock HTTP server or request capture ensures the actual serialized payload includes all labels in the correct format.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a mock HTTP server or request interceptor. Call classify() with all expected taxonomy labels in the system config. Capture the HTTP request body and verify it contains all taxonomy labels as expected (correct field names, correct order if applicable, proper JSON/protobuf encoding).

behaviors.description: When the API returns a label that is not in the taxonomy, the module returns 'unknown' instead of forwarding the unexpected value to the caller.
behaviors.justification: This is pure input validation and control-flow logic. The real failure is that the module forwards an invalid label (e.g., 'bogus') to the caller instead of substituting 'unknown'. A unit test with a mocked API that returns an out-of-taxonomy label will definitively catch this. The mock is not hiding any failure—the logic is deterministic and local. No external dependencies or serialization concerns.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API client to return a label ('unexpected_label') that is not in the configured taxonomy. Call classify() and assert that it returns 'unknown', not the invalid label. Also verify the returned result is exactly the string 'unknown' (case-sensitive).

behaviors.description: The classification result for the same document is the same across repeated calls.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that repeated calls with the same document return different results—indicating either broken caching or an unhandled non-deterministic API. A unit test can catch this by mocking the API client to return *different* values each time it's called, then verifying that calling classify(doc) twice returns the same result and that the API mock was called only once (proving caching worked). This mock does not hide failures; it simulates real non-determinism and verifies the cache prevents it.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API client to return different results on successive calls (e.g., 'legal' on first call, 'technical' on second). Call classify(doc) twice with the same document. Assert that both results are identical and that the API mock was called exactly once (using mock.call_count or assert_called_once_with).

behaviors.description: The number of tokens consumed for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that tokens consumed exceed the budget due to incorrect token counting, incorrect budget comparison, or mishandling of the API's token response. A unit test can verify this by: (1) mocking the API to return a known token count, (2) calling classify() with a document, (3) asserting that the operation succeeds when tokens ≤ budget and fails/rejects when tokens > budget. If the module uses a deterministic tokenizer library (e.g., tiktoken), that library can be called directly in the test. The mock-based approach doesn't hide failures in the budget enforcement logic.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the API to return responses with different token counts (e.g., 100 tokens, 1000 tokens). Configure the module with a per-call budget (e.g., 500 tokens). Call classify() and assert: (1) when tokens ≤ budget, operation succeeds and returns the label; (2) when tokens > budget, operation is rejected with an appropriate error or returns a sentinel value without calling the API. If the module does its own token counting, call the tokenizer on a test document and verify the count is within expected bounds.

behaviors.description: When the API signals that the request rate is too high, the module waits and retries rather than returning an error to the caller.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that the module does not retry on rate limit errors, or retries incorrectly (wrong backoff, wrong retry count). A unit test with a mocked API can catch this by configuring the mock to return a rate-limit error on the first call and a success on the second call, then verifying the final result is success. The timing/backoff logic can be tested by mocking the sleep/time functions so the test doesn't hang. The mock is not hiding failures—it verifies the core control flow and retry logic work correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the API client to raise a RateLimitError on the first call, then return a valid label on the second call. Mock the sleep/time.sleep() function to avoid real delays. Call classify() and assert: (1) the final result is the label from the second call (not an error), (2) the API mock was called at least twice (proving retry happened), (3) sleep was called at least once (proving wait happened). Optionally verify backoff: sleep was called with increasing durations or expected durations.
